situations.title: Greetings & Presence
situations.summary: Automate the greeting loop. Essential for every social interaction in Madeira.

variants.note: A common Madeiran way to agree.

goals: Master the basic greeting loop
Understand the "Tudo bem" response pattern
Practice pronunciation of nasal vowels

cultural_notes.title: Greetings & Presence
cultural_notes.body: In Madeira, greetings are more than just words; they are a social ritual. Always respond to "Tudo bem?" with "Tudo bem, obrigado/a" before asking back.

cultural_notes.title: The Importance of Greetings
cultural_notes.body: In Madeira, as in most of Portugal, a proper greeting is essential before starting any interaction, even a quick one. It's considered polite to always say 'Bom dia', 'Boa tarde', or 'Boa noite' when entering a shop, restaurant, or even just passing someone on a path. Skipping this step can be seen as impolite or abrupt.

cultural_notes.title: 'Tudo bem?' as a Common Check-in
cultural_notes.body: The phrase 'Tudo bem?' (Everything OK?) is more than just a question; it's a common social ritual. It's often used as part of a greeting and is usually responded to positively, even if things aren't perfectly 'bem'. It shows care and acknowledgment, and it's polite to always ask it back to the other person.

dialogues.title: Morning Coffee Greeting
dialogues.context: At a small café in Funchal, a customer greets the barista.

lines.speaker: Barista
lines.text: Bom dia! Tudo bem?
lines.translation: Good morning! Everything OK?

lines.speaker: Cliente
lines.text: Bom dia! Tudo bem, obrigado. E você?
lines.translation: Good morning! Everything OK, thank you. And you?

lines.speaker: Barista
lines.text: Tudo bem, sim. P'ra já!
lines.translation: Everything OK, yes. Right away!

dialogues.title: Neighbours Meeting
dialogues.context: Two neighbours meet in the street in the afternoon.

lines.speaker: Vizinha A
lines.text: Boa tarde, Maria! Tudo bem?
lines.translation: Good afternoon, Maria! Everything OK?

lines.speaker: Vizinha B
lines.text: Boa tarde, Dona Fátima! Tudo bem, pois é. E a senhora?
lines.translation: Good afternoon, Mrs. Fátima! Everything OK, indeed. And you?

lines.speaker: Vizinha A
lines.text: Tudo bem, querida. Tenha uma boa tarde!
lines.translation: Everything OK, dear. Have a good afternoon!

roleplay.scenario: You enter a small shop in Madeira to buy something. The shopkeeper greets you.

nodes.npc_text: Bom dia! Tudo bem?
nodes.npc_translation: Good morning! Everything OK?

options.text: Bom dia! Tudo bem, obrigado. E o senhor?
options.translation: Good morning! Everything OK, thank you. And you?
options.feedback: Excellent! You used a polite greeting and asked back.

options.text: Olá! Tudo bem.
options.translation: Hello! Everything OK.
options.feedback: Good, but a bit informal for a first greeting in a shop. You could also ask how they are.

options.text: Tenha um bom dia!
options.translation: Have a good day!
options.feedback: That's a goodbye! You need to respond to their 'Tudo bem?' first.

nodes.npc_text: Tudo bem, obrigado. Em que posso ajudar?
nodes.npc_translation: Everything OK, thank you. How can I help you?

options.text: Obrigado.
options.translation: Thank you.
options.feedback: You successfully completed the greeting loop! Now you can ask for what you need.

nodes.npc_text: Ah, tudo bem. Em que posso ajudar?
nodes.npc_translation: Ah, everything OK. How can I help you?

options.text: Obrigado.
options.translation: Thank you.
options.feedback: You've responded, but next time try to ask back 'E o senhor?' to be more polite.

nodes.npc_text: Ah... Bom dia para si também.
nodes.npc_translation: Ah... Good morning to you too.

options.text: Desculpe.
options.translation: Sorry.
options.feedback: It's okay! Remember to respond to 'Tudo bem?' before saying goodbye.

situations.title: Ordering in a Café
situations.summary: Learn to order coffee, bread, and pastries in a local snack bar.

variants.text: Queria um sumo de laranja, por favor.
variants.translation: I'd like an orange juice, please.

variants.text: Uma meia de leite, se faz favor.

goals: Order coffee and snacks confidently
Learn the names of local coffee variations
Practice polite requests using "Queria"

cultural_notes.title: Ordering in a Café
cultural_notes.body: Ordering coffee in Madeira is specific. A "bica" is your standard espresso. If you want a bit of milk, ask for a "garoto".

cultural_notes.title: Coffee Names in Portugal
cultural_notes.body: While 'café' generally refers to an espresso throughout Portugal, in Madeira, locals often use 'bica' instead. Both terms are understood, but using 'bica' will make you sound more local. Other common coffee types include 'meia de leite' (half coffee, half milk) and 'galão' (a larger milk coffee, served in a tall glass).

cultural_notes.title: Politeness in Ordering
cultural_notes.body: In Portugal, politeness is highly valued. Always remember to use 'por favor' or 'se faz favor' (both meaning 'please') when making a request. 'Obrigado' (if you're male) or 'Obrigada' (if you're female) for 'thank you' is also essential when receiving your order or the bill.

dialogues.title: Ordering Coffee and Pastry
dialogues.context: A customer orders an espresso and a pastry at a local café.

lines.speaker: Cliente
lines.text: Bom dia! Queria uma bica e um pastel de nata, por favor.
lines.translation: Good morning! I'd like an espresso and a custard tart, please.

lines.speaker: Empregado
lines.text: Pois, uma bica e um pastel. É p'ra beber aqui?
lines.translation: Right, an espresso and a tart. Is it to drink here?

lines.speaker: Cliente
lines.text: Sim, é p'ra beber aqui, obrigado.
lines.translation: Yes, it's to drink here, thank you.

lines.speaker: Empregado
lines.text: Tá bem. Mais alguma coisa?
lines.translation: Okay. Anything else?

lines.speaker: Cliente
lines.text: Não, obrigado. A conta, por favor.
lines.translation: No, thank you. The bill, please.

lines.speaker: Empregado
lines.text: São dois euros e cinquenta.
lines.translation: That's two euros and fifty cents.

dialogues.title: Ordering Different Drinks
dialogues.context: A customer orders a 'galão' and an orange juice.

lines.speaker: Cliente
lines.text: Olá. Queria um galão e um sumo de laranja, por favor.
lines.translation: Hello. I'd like a 'galão' and an orange juice, please.

lines.speaker: Empregado
lines.text: Um galão e um sumo. P'ra já!
lines.translation: A 'galão' and a juice. Coming right up!

lines.speaker: Cliente
lines.text: Obrigado.
lines.translation: Thank you.

lines.speaker: Empregado
lines.text: Aqui tem.
lines.translation: Here you go.

lines.speaker: Cliente
lines.text: Quanto é?
lines.translation: How much is it?

lines.speaker: Empregado
lines.text: Três euros e vinte.
lines.translation: Three euros and twenty cents.

situations.title: Numbers 1-10 + Connectors
situations.summary: Master basic numbers and start connecting your thoughts with "e", "mas", "porque".

variants.text: Queria um café e uma água.
variants.translation: I'd like one coffee and one water.

variants.text: Queria três sumos, mas só tenho cinco euros.
variants.translation: I'd like three juices, but I only have five euros.

variants.text: Queria quatro bolos, porque gosto muito.
variants.translation: I'd like four cakes, because I like them a lot.

goals: Count to 10 fluently
Use basic conjunctions to form longer sentences
Understand prices in shops

cultural_notes.title: Numbers 1-10 + Connectors
cultural_notes.body: Numbers are the backbone of daily transactions. Connectors like "porque" help you explain your choices, making you sound more natural.

cultural_notes.title: Counting with Fingers in Portugal
cultural_notes.body: When indicating numbers with your fingers in Portugal, especially 1, 2, and 3, it's common to start with your thumb for one, thumb and index for two, and so on. This can sometimes differ from other cultures where the index finger might be 'one'.

cultural_notes.title: Using 'e' for 'and'
cultural_notes.body: The conjunction 'e' (and) is essential for linking items in a list or combining simple ideas. It's often used when ordering multiple items in a shop or café, like 'um café e um bolo' (one coffee and one cake).

dialogues.title: Ordering at the café
dialogues.context: A tourist is ordering coffee and a pastry at a café in Funchal.

lines.speaker: Empregado
lines.text: Bom dia! O que deseja?
lines.translation: Good morning! What would you like?

lines.speaker: Turista
lines.text: Bom dia. Queria dois cafés e um bolo de arroz, por favor.
lines.translation: Good morning. I'd like two coffees and a rice cake, please.

lines.speaker: Empregado
lines.text: Dois cafés e um bolo de arroz. Mais alguma coisa?
lines.translation: Two coffees and a rice cake. Anything else?

lines.speaker: Turista
lines.text: Não, só isso. Quanto é?
lines.translation: No, just that. How much is it?

lines.speaker: Empregado
lines.text: São três euros e um bocado. Quatro euros, no total.
lines.translation: It's three euros and a bit. Four euros, in total.

dialogues.title: Buying fruit
dialogues.context: A local is buying fruit at the market, asking about prices and quantities.

lines.speaker: Vendedor
lines.text: Diga! Quer o quê?
lines.translation: Yes! What do you want?

lines.speaker: Cliente
lines.text: Bom dia. Queria cinco bananas, mas não muito grandes.
lines.translation: Good morning. I'd like five bananas, but not too big.

lines.speaker: Vendedor
lines.text: Cinco bananas. Certo. E mais alguma coisa?
lines.translation: Five bananas. Alright. And anything else?

lines.speaker: Cliente
lines.text: Sim, e três maçãs, por favor, porque gosto muito de maçãs.
lines.translation: Yes, and three apples, please, because I really like apples.

lines.speaker: Vendedor
lines.text: Cinco bananas e três maçãs. São sete euros.
lines.translation: Five bananas and three apples. That's seven euros.

roleplay.scenario: You are at a small shop in Madeira, buying some items. You need to ask for quantities and understand prices.

nodes.npc_text: Bom dia! O que deseja?
nodes.npc_translation: Good morning! What would you like?

options.text: Bom dia. Queria um sumo e dois bolos.
options.translation: Good morning. I'd like one juice and two cakes.
options.feedback: Great start! You asked for two items using 'e'.

options.text: Queria três águas, mas pequenas.
options.translation: I'd like three waters, but small ones.
options.feedback: Good use of 'mas' to specify.

nodes.npc_text: Um sumo e dois bolos. Certo. São cinco euros.
nodes.npc_translation: One juice and two cakes. Right. That's five euros.

options.text: Cinco euros? É um bocado.
options.translation: Five euros? That's a bit much.
options.feedback: You expressed surprise about the price using 'um bocado'.

options.text: Ok, aqui está. Obrigado.
options.translation: Okay, here you go. Thank you.
options.feedback: Polite and direct ending to the transaction.

nodes.npc_text: Três águas pequenas. Ótimo. São quatro euros.
nodes.npc_translation: Three small waters. Great. That's four euros.

options.text: Quatro euros. E queria mais um café, por favor.
options.translation: Four euros. And I'd like one more coffee, please.
options.feedback: You added another item using 'e'.

options.text: Então, só as águas. Obrigado.
options.translation: So, just the waters. Thank you.
options.feedback: You chose to buy only the initial items.

nodes.npc_text: Pois é. Os preços sobem. Quer só um bolo, então?
nodes.npc_translation: That's how it is. Prices go up. Do you want just one cake, then?

options.text: Sim, só um bolo, porque é mais barato.
options.translation: Yes, just one cake, because it's cheaper.
options.feedback: You used 'porque' to explain your choice.

options.text: Não, está bem. Dois bolos e um sumo.
options.translation: No, it's fine. Two cakes and one juice.
options.feedback: You decided to stick with your original order.

nodes.npc_text: Mais um café. Então, três águas e um café. São cinco euros e um bocado.
nodes.npc_translation: One more coffee. So, three waters and one coffee. That's five euros and a bit.

options.text: Ok, são seis euros. Aqui está.
options.translation: Okay, that's six euros. Here you go.
options.feedback: You understood the total and paid.

situations.title: Confusion & Clarification
situations.summary: Build your escape hatch. Learn how to handle fast speech and ask for repetition.

variants.note: Can sound abrupt if not used carefully.

variants.text: Pode dizer de novo, por favor?
variants.translation: Can you say it again, please?

variants.text: Fale mais devagar, se faz favor.

dialogues.title: Asking for directions
dialogues.context: A tourist asks a shop assistant for directions to a specific street.

lines.speaker: Turista
lines.text: Olá. Onde é a Rua da Carreira?
lines.translation: Hello. Where is Carreira Street?

lines.speaker: Assistente
lines.text: É aqui à direita, depois segue sempre em frente e vira na segunda à esquerda.
lines.translation: It's here to the right, then you go straight ahead and turn on the second left.

lines.speaker: Turista
lines.text: Não percebi. Pode repetir?
lines.translation: I didn't understand. Can you repeat?

lines.speaker: Assistente
lines.text: Claro. Vá por aqui, à direita. Depois, siga em frente. Na segunda rua, vira à esquerda.
lines.translation: Of course. Go this way, to the right. Then, go straight. On the second street, turn left.

lines.speaker: Turista
lines.text: Ah, percebi. Obrigado!
lines.translation: Ah, I understood. Thank you!

dialogues.title: Ordering a coffee
dialogues.context: A local barista speaks quickly to a tourist trying to order a coffee.

lines.speaker: Turista
lines.text: Um café, se faz favor.
lines.translation: A coffee, please.

lines.speaker: Barista
lines.text: É p'ra beber aqui ou p'ra levar? Com açúcar ou sem? Quer um bolo do caco?
lines.translation: Is it to drink here or to take away? With sugar or without? Do you want a bolo do caco?

lines.speaker: Turista
lines.text: Diz? Mais devagar, se faz favor.
lines.translation: Pardon? More slowly, please.

lines.speaker: Barista
lines.text: Ah, desculpe. P'ra beber aqui? Ou p'ra levar?
lines.translation: Oh, sorry. To drink here? Or to take away?

lines.speaker: Turista
lines.text: P'ra beber aqui.
lines.translation: To drink here.

roleplay.scenario: You are at a local market in Funchal, trying to buy some fruit. The vendor speaks a bit fast.

nodes.npc_text: Bom dia! Posso ajudar? Temos umas bananas da Madeira ótimas hoje!
nodes.npc_translation: Good morning! Can I help? We have some great Madeiran bananas today!

options.text: Bom dia. Queria um quilo de bananas, se faz favor.
options.translation: Good morning. I'd like a kilo of bananas, please.
options.feedback: Great start, polite and clear.

options.text: Olá. Bananas, por favor.
options.translation: Hello. Bananas, please.
options.feedback: A bit direct, but understandable. Try to add 'queria um quilo'.

nodes.npc_text: Um quilo? Ok! Quer também umas maracujás ou umas anonas? Estão fresquinhas!
nodes.npc_translation: A kilo? Okay! Do you also want some passion fruit or some custard apples? They're very fresh!

options.text: Diz? Não percebi.
options.translation: Pardon? I didn't understand.
options.feedback: Excellent, using your escape hatch!

options.text: Mais devagar, se faz favor.
options.translation: More slowly, please.
options.feedback: Good, asking for slower speech.

nodes.npc_text: Ah, desculpe! Perguntei se queria maracujás ou anonas. São frutas da ilha.
nodes.npc_translation: Oh, sorry! I asked if you wanted passion fruit or custard apples. They are island fruits.

options.text: Não, obrigado. Só as bananas.
options.translation: No, thank you. Just the bananas.
options.feedback: Clear and polite refusal.

options.text: Sim, uma maracujá, se faz favor.
options.translation: Yes, one passion fruit, please.
options.feedback: Good, you understood and added to your order.

nodes.npc_text: Muito bem. São três euros e cinquenta. Obrigado e volte sempre!
nodes.npc_translation: Very good. That's three euros fifty. Thank you and come back often!

options.text: Obrigado! Adeus.
options.translation: Thank you! Goodbye.
options.feedback: Perfect ending to the interaction.

mission.title: Ask for Repetition at the Market
prep: Imagine you are at a fruit stall in a Madeiran market.
Practice saying 'Diz?' and 'Mais devagar, se faz favor.'
Think about what you might want to buy (e.g., 'um quilo de bananas', 'duas maçãs').

cultural_notes.title: Politeness in Asking for Repetition
cultural_notes.body: While 'Diz?' or 'Como?' are common informal ways to ask for repetition, adding 'se faz favor' or 'por favor' (please) makes the request more polite and is generally appreciated, especially with strangers or in service situations. 'Não percebi' is a very clear and neutral way to indicate lack of understanding.

cultural_notes.title: Paciência: A Madeiran Trait
cultural_notes.body: The word 'Paciência' (patience) is not just a vocabulary item; it reflects a common attitude in Madeira. People are generally understanding if you need them to speak more slowly or repeat themselves, especially with tourists. Don't be afraid to use your 'escape hatch' phrases.

situations.title: Locations & Directions
situations.summary: Navigate Madeira. Ask where things are and understand "em cima" and "em baixo".

variants.note: More formal way to say 'Go straight ahead'.
Informal version of 'Vire à esquerda'.

dialogues.title: Asking for the market
dialogues.context: A tourist asks a local for directions to the market.

lines.speaker: Turista
lines.text: Com licença, onde fica o mercado?
lines.translation: Excuse me, where is the market?

lines.speaker: Local
lines.text: O mercado? Pois, é ali. Vire à direita na esquina, depois sempre em frente.
lines.translation: The market? Ah, it's there. Turn right at the corner, then straight ahead.

lines.speaker: Turista
lines.text: Sempre em frente?
lines.translation: Straight ahead?

lines.speaker: Local
lines.text: Isso mesmo. É um bocado em baixo.
lines.translation: That's right. It's a bit downhill.

dialogues.title: Finding a pharmacy
dialogues.context: A tourist asks a shop assistant for a pharmacy.

lines.speaker: Turista
lines.text: Bom dia. Desculpe, onde fica a farmácia?
lines.translation: Good morning. Excuse me, where is the pharmacy?

lines.speaker: Assistente
lines.text: Bom dia. A farmácia? Fica em cima, aqui perto.
lines.translation: Good morning. The pharmacy? It's uphill, near here.

lines.speaker: Turista
lines.text: Em cima?
lines.translation: Uphill?

lines.speaker: Assistente
lines.text: Sim, vire à esquerda, depois é a primeira à direita. É fácil.
lines.translation: Yes, turn left, then it's the first on the right. It's easy.

roleplay.scenario: You are in Funchal and need to find a bus stop to go to the botanical garden. Ask a local person for directions.

nodes.npc_text: Bom dia! Posso ajudar?
nodes.npc_translation: Good morning! Can I help you?

options.text: Onde fica a paragem de autocarro para o Jardim Botânico?
options.translation: Where is the bus stop for the Botanical Garden?
options.feedback: Great start, asking directly for the bus stop!

options.text: Onde é o Jardim Botânico?
options.translation: Where is the Botanical Garden?
options.feedback: You'll get directions to the garden, but not necessarily the bus stop.

nodes.npc_text: Ah, para o Jardim Botânico... Pois, a paragem fica ali, em cima. Vire à direita, depois sempre em frente, na próxima esquina.
nodes.npc_translation: Ah, for the Botanical Garden... Well, the stop is there, uphill. Turn right, then straight ahead, at the next corner.

options.text: Em cima e à direita?
options.translation: Uphill and to the right?
options.feedback: Good job confirming the directions!

options.text: Fica aqui perto?
options.translation: Is it near here?
options.feedback: A good question to gauge the walkability.

nodes.npc_text: O Jardim Botânico é um bocado longe, mas o autocarro vai lá. A paragem é ali, em cima.
nodes.npc_translation: The Botanical Garden is a bit far, but the bus goes there. The stop is there, uphill.

options.text: Onde fica a paragem de autocarro?
options.translation: Where is the bus stop?
options.feedback: Good, you clarified for the bus stop specifically.

nodes.npc_text: Isso mesmo. Em cima, à direita, e depois sempre em frente.
nodes.npc_translation: That's right. Uphill, to the right, and then straight ahead.

options.text: Muito obrigado!
options.translation: Thank you very much!
options.feedback: Polite ending, well done!

nodes.npc_text: É um bocado longe para ir a pé, mas de autocarro é rápido.
nodes.npc_translation: It's a bit far to go on foot, but by bus it's quick.

options.text: Muito obrigado!
options.translation: Thank you very much!
options.feedback: Polite ending, well done!

nodes.npc_text: Vire à direita, depois sempre em frente, na próxima esquina. É em cima.

cultural_notes.title: Politeness in Asking for Directions
cultural_notes.body: In Portugal, it's customary to start your request for directions with a polite phrase like 'Com licença' (Excuse me) or 'Desculpe' (Sorry/Excuse me) before asking your question. This shows respect and acknowledges that you're interrupting someone.

cultural_notes.title: Madeiran Directions: Uphill and Downhill
cultural_notes.body: Given Funchal's hilly landscape, directions often include 'em cima' (uphill) and 'em baixo' (downhill). These aren't just about elevation but often imply a general direction relative to the main road or sea. Pay attention to these terms as they are crucial for navigating the city.

mission.title: Share your opinion on a local dish
prep: Practice saying 'Gosto imenso!', 'Parece-me bem.', and 'Acho que é uma maravilha!'.
Think about something you've tried in Madeira (like 'bolo do caco' or 'poncha').

cultural_notes.title: Softening opinions with 'Parece-me'
cultural_notes.body: While 'Acho que...' (I think that...) is a direct way to express an opinion, 'Parece-me que...' (It seems to me that...) or simply 'Parece-me bem.' (It seems good to me.) is a slightly softer and often more polite way to share your thoughts, especially when you're not entirely certain or want to be less assertive. It's very common in everyday conversation.

cultural_notes.title: The versatility of 'Pois é!'
cultural_notes.body: 'Pois é!' is a very common and versatile interjection in Portuguese. It can mean 'That's right!', 'Exactly!', 'Indeed!', or simply be a way to acknowledge what someone has said, showing agreement or understanding. It's often used to keep a conversation flowing naturally.

cultural_notes.title: Bica vs. Café
cultural_notes.body: In Portugal, especially in Madeira, if you ask for 'um café', you will almost always receive an espresso. Many locals use the term 'uma bica' interchangeably with 'um café' to refer to an espresso. If you want a longer coffee, like an Americano, you'll need to specify 'um abatanado' or 'um café cheio'.

cultural_notes.title: Politeness in Transactions
cultural_notes.body: Politeness is highly valued in daily interactions. Always use 'por favor' (please) when asking for something and 'obrigado/a' (thank you) when receiving. 'Com licença' (excuse me) is used to get someone's attention or to pass by someone in a crowded space. These small words make a big difference.

mission.title: Introduce Yourself to a Local
prep: Practice saying your name: 'Chamo-me [o seu nome]'.
Practice saying your origin: 'Sou de [o seu país/cidade]'.
Practice saying your reason for being in Madeira: 'Estou cá há [tempo]' OR 'Sou reformado/a' OR 'Trabalho cá'.

cultural_notes.title: Muito prazer vs. Prazer
cultural_notes.body: When introduced to someone, 'Muito prazer' (pronounced 'moo-ee-toh pra-ZER') is the polite and common phrase, meaning 'Nice to meet you' or 'Much pleasure'. While 'Prazer' alone is sometimes used, 'Muito prazer' is generally preferred as it is more complete and formal, especially in a first encounter.

cultural_notes.title: Addressing People: 'Tu' vs. 'O Senhor / A Senhora'
cultural_notes.body: In Portugal, 'tu' is used for informal address with friends, family, and peers. For strangers, especially older people or those in service roles (like shopkeepers, receptionists), it's more polite to use the third person singular with 'o senhor' (for a man) or 'a senhora' (for a woman). For example, instead of 'Como te chamas?' (informal), you'd say 'Como se chama o senhor/a senhora?' or simply 'Como se chama?' (using the polite 3rd person form). At L0, it's safer to default to the polite form with strangers until you are invited to use 'tu'.

mission.title: Find out when the market opens
prep: Practice asking: 'Bom dia. Que horas abre o mercado?' (Good morning. What time does the market open?)
Be ready to understand times like 'nove e meia' (nine thirty) or 'oito da manhã' (eight in the morning).
Practice thanking them: 'Muito obrigado!'
likely_responses: Bom dia! Abre às sete da manhã.
Abre às oito e meia, de Segunda a Sábado.

cultural_notes.title: Expressing Half-Hours
cultural_notes.body: In Portugal, when telling time, 'meia' (half) is commonly used after the hour, like 'dez e meia' (half past ten). It's less common to say 'dez e trinta' (ten thirty) in everyday speech, though it's understood.

cultural_notes.title: Asking for the Time Politely
cultural_notes.body: When asking a stranger for the time, it's polite to start with 'Desculpe' (Excuse me) or 'Com licença' (Excuse me/Pardon me) before 'Que horas são?' (What time is it?). This shows respect and acknowledges you're interrupting them.

cultural_notes.title: Multibanco: The Portuguese Payment System
cultural_notes.body: In Portugal, 'Multibanco' refers to the national interbank network of ATMs and payment terminals. When you ask 'Pode pagar com cartão?', shopkeepers often reply with 'Multibanco' to confirm that card payments are accepted via this system. It's a very common and efficient way to pay, often preferred over cash for larger amounts.

cultural_notes.title: Using 'Pois' and 'Pois é'
cultural_notes.body: In Portugal, 'Pois' or 'Pois é' are very common interjections used to express agreement, acknowledgement, or to confirm something. They can often be translated as 'Indeed', 'That's right', or simply 'Yes' in context, but carry a slightly more emphatic or reflective tone than a simple 'Sim'.

cultural_notes.title: The Versatility of 'Fazer'
cultural_notes.body: The verb 'fazer' (to do/make) is extremely common. In the past tense ('Eu fiz'), it can be used for a wide range of activities, from 'fazer uma caminhada' (to go for a walk) to 'fazer compras' (to do shopping) or 'fazer exercício' (to exercise).

mission.title: Describe Your Surroundings
prep: Look around you and identify 2-3 things (e.g., a drink, the weather, a person).
Think about one permanent quality (ser) and one temporary state (estar) for each.
Practice saying sentences like 'O café é bom.' and 'Está fresco.'

cultural_notes.title: Ser vs. Estar: More Than Permanent vs. Temporary
cultural_notes.body: While 'ser' often implies permanence and 'estar' implies temporary states, the distinction is more nuanced. For adjectives like 'bom' (good) or 'mau' (bad), 'ser' describes an inherent quality ('O bolo do caco é bom' - The bolo do caco is good by nature/recipe), while 'estar' describes a condition ('O bolo do caco está bom' - The bolo do caco is good right now, e.g., fresh, warm, or tastes good at this moment). For weather, 'estar' is always used: 'Está fresco' (It's cool).

cultural_notes.title: Expressing Agreement: 'Pois é' and 'Sim, é verdade'
cultural_notes.body: In Madeira and Portugal, it's very common to use 'Pois é' (pronounced 'poish eh') or 'Sim, é verdade' ('Yes, it's true') to agree with someone's observation. 'Pois é' is particularly frequent and can also express a sense of 'indeed' or 'that's right,' often with a slight pause or nod. These are natural and friendly ways to participate in a conversation.

lines.text: Ah, às sete. E depois? Tomo o pequeno-almoço cedo.
lines.translation: Ah, at seven. And then? I have breakfast early.

cultural_notes.title: The importance of 'pequeno-almoço'
cultural_notes.body: In Portugal, 'pequeno-almoço' (breakfast) is a significant meal, often taken at home with family, or at a local 'pastelaria' or 'café'. It's more than just a quick bite; it's a social ritual for many, especially when enjoyed out.

cultural_notes.title: Using 'pois' for agreement
cultural_notes.body: You'll often hear 'pois' or 'pois é' in Portugal. It's a very common interjection used to express agreement, understanding, or to acknowledge what someone has said, similar to 'indeed' or 'that's right' in English. It adds a natural, conversational flow.

cultural_notes.title: Asking for 'the bill'
cultural_notes.body: In Portugal, the most common and polite way to ask for the bill is 'A conta, por favor.' (The bill, please.). While 'Pode trazer a conta?' (Can you bring the bill?) is also correct, the shorter version is more frequently used in casual settings like cafés and restaurants.

cultural_notes.title: The 'Bica' vs. 'Café'
cultural_notes.body: While 'café' is understood everywhere, in Lisbon and southern Portugal, a standard espresso is often called a 'bica'. In Madeira and northern Portugal, it's more commonly referred to simply as 'um café'. Both are correct, but using 'um café' is universally safe and natural in Madeira.

situations.title: Talking About Others
situations.summary: Master the third person (ele/ela/eles/elas) and irregular past forms.

variants.text: Os meus vizinhos chamam-se Ana e Carlos.
variants.translation: My neighbours are called Ana and Carlos.

goals: Describe other people and their activities
Use third-person singular and plural correctly
Master key irregular preterite forms

cultural_notes.title: Talking About Others
cultural_notes.body: In Portugal, "ele" and "ela" are used freely, but often dropped when context is clear. "Foi" can mean both "he/she went" and "he/she was".

cultural_notes.title: The Importance of Neighbourhood in Madeira
cultural_notes.body: In Madeira, as in many parts of Portugal, knowing your neighbours (vizinhos) and their daily routines is a common part of social life. People often greet each other, exchange pleasantries, and sometimes share information about local happenings. This creates a strong sense of community, especially in smaller towns and villages.

cultural_notes.title: Using 'o senhor' / 'a senhora' for Politeness
cultural_notes.body: When addressing or referring to adults you don't know well, or those older than you, it's common and polite to use 'o senhor' (Mr.) or 'a senhora' (Mrs./Ms.) before their name or as a standalone form of address. For example, 'A senhora Maria' or 'O senhor João'. This is a sign of respect and is widely used in daily interactions, particularly in shops, cafés, or with public officials.

situations.title: TER: Have, Need, Feel
situations.summary: Master the verb "ter" for possession, obligation, and physical states.

variants.text: Tenho uma dor de cabeça forte.
Preciso de um café, por favor.

goals: Express physical sensations and needs
Use "ter que" for obligations
Distinguish between "ter" and "precisar de"

cultural_notes.title: TER: Have, Need, Feel
cultural_notes.body: "Estou constipado/a" means you have a cold. It is a very common false friend for English speakers!

cultural_notes.title: Expressing physical states: 'Ter' vs 'Estar com'
cultural_notes.body: In European Portuguese, when expressing physical sensations like hunger, thirst, cold, or pain, we almost always use the verb 'ter' (to have) followed by the noun. For example, 'Tenho fome' (I have hunger = I'm hungry) is standard. Saying 'Estou com fome' (I am with hunger) is more common in Brazilian Portuguese and sounds less natural in Portugal.

cultural_notes.title: 'Ter que' vs 'Ter de' for obligation
cultural_notes.body: Both 'ter que' and 'ter de' are used to express obligation in European Portuguese, meaning 'to have to' or 'must'. They are largely interchangeable in everyday speech, especially in informal contexts. So, 'Tenho que ir' and 'Tenho de ir' both mean 'I have to go'.

media.caption: Essential Guide to the Portuguese verb 'TER' (European Portuguese)

situations.title: PODER & Polite Requests
situations.summary: Use "poder" for permission, offers, and polite requests.

variants.note: Offer to a stranger
Offer to a friend

variants.translation: May I sit next to you?

variants.note: Offering help
Offering help politely

variants.translation: Can you give me a bica (espresso)?

goals: Make polite requests and ask for permission
Offer help to others
Distinguish between "poder" and "conseguir"

cultural_notes.title: PODER & Polite Requests
cultural_notes.body: "Pode" is the standard polite way to ask for things in Portugal. It is not rude; it is the engine of politeness.

cultural_notes.title: The importance of 'por favor' and 'com licença'
cultural_notes.body: In Portugal, especially in service interactions, adding 'por favor' (please) to requests is essential for politeness. 'Com licença' (excuse me) is used to get someone's attention before making a request or asking for permission, particularly if they are busy or if you are interrupting.

cultural_notes.title: Addressing others with 'Pode' (formal) vs. 'Podes' (informal)
cultural_notes.body: When using 'poder' for requests, 'Pode' (you can/may, formal) is the standard for addressing strangers, shopkeepers, or anyone you'd show respect to. If you are speaking to a friend or someone you know well, you would use 'Podes' (you can/may, informal). At L1, focusing on 'Pode' is generally safer for polite requests.

cultural_notes.title: Shopping: Clothes & Colours
cultural_notes.body: Colours must agree with the gender of the noun. "Uma camisola vermelha" but "Um casaco vermelho".

cultural_notes.title: Asking for help in shops
cultural_notes.body: In Portugal, to get a shop assistant's attention, you can say 'Diga!' (Tell me!) or 'Faz favor!' (Please!). When addressing them, it's common to use the null subject (implying 'you' politely) or 'o senhor / a senhora' rather than 'você', which can sound too direct or even distancing in some contexts. For example, 'Precisa de ajuda?' (Do you need help?) is more common than 'Você precisa de ajuda?'.

cultural_notes.title: Trying on clothes
cultural_notes.body: When shopping for clothes, if you want to try something on, you'll ask for 'o provador' (the fitting room). It's expected that you use the designated changing areas rather than trying things on in the open shop floor.

situations.title: Health & Pharmacy
situations.summary: Describe symptoms, understand dosages, and handle pharmacy visits.

variants.translation: I have pain in my throat.
I have an allergy to pollen.
How often should I take it?

variants.text: Quantas vezes por dia devo tomar?
variants.translation: How many times a day should I take it?

goals: Describe physical symptoms and pain
Understand medication instructions and dosages
Explain allergies and medical history

cultural_notes.title: Health & Pharmacy
cultural_notes.body: "Dói-me a cabeça" literally means "the head hurts me". This is the most natural way to express pain in Portuguese.

cultural_notes.title: Pharmacies as a First Stop
cultural_notes.body: In Portugal, pharmacies often serve as a first point of contact for minor health issues. Pharmacists are highly trained and can provide advice and over-the-counter medication for common ailments, sometimes even suggesting a doctor's visit if necessary. It's common to describe your symptoms directly to the pharmacist.

cultural_notes.title: Politeness in Service Interactions
cultural_notes.body: When interacting with service staff, like a pharmacist, it's polite to use 'o senhor'/'a senhora' (the formal 'you') or simply use the third person singular with verbs (e.g., 'Em que posso ajudar?' meaning 'How can I help?' rather than 'How can I help you?'). Always start with a 'Bom dia'/'Boa tarde' and end with an 'Obrigado/a'.

dialogues.title: At the Pharmacy for a Sore Throat
dialogues.context: A person goes to a pharmacy in Madeira to get something for a sore throat.

lines.speaker: Farmacêutico
lines.text: Boa tarde! Em que posso ajudar?
lines.translation: Good afternoon! How can I help?

lines.speaker: Cliente
lines.text: Boa tarde. Dói-me a garganta, um bocado.
lines.translation: Good afternoon. My throat hurts, a little.

lines.speaker: Farmacêutico
lines.text: Pois, e tem mais algum sintoma? Febre, tosse?
lines.translation: Right, and do you have any other symptoms? Fever, cough?

lines.speaker: Cliente
lines.text: Não, só a garganta. E tenho um bocado de tosse, sim.
lines.translation: No, just my throat. And I have a bit of a cough, yes.

lines.speaker: Farmacêutico
lines.text: OK. Tenho aqui um spray e uns comprimidos para a garganta. Quer ver?
lines.translation: Okay. I have a spray and some throat lozenges here. Would you like to see?

dialogues.title: Understanding Medication Instructions
dialogues.context: A customer is asking a pharmacist about how to take a new medication.

lines.speaker: Cliente
lines.text: Desculpe, este comprimido, quantas vezes por dia devo tomar?
lines.translation: Excuse me, this tablet, how many times a day should I take it?

lines.speaker: Farmacêutico
lines.text: Deve tomar um comprimido, duas vezes por dia.
lines.translation: You should take one tablet, twice a day.

lines.speaker: Cliente
lines.text: E é antes ou depois das refeições?
lines.translation: And is it before or after meals?

lines.speaker: Farmacêutico
lines.text: É depois das refeições, com um copo de água.
lines.translation: It's after meals, with a glass of water.

lines.speaker: Cliente
lines.text: Pois, obrigada!
lines.translation: Right, thank you!

roleplay.scenario: You are at a pharmacy in Funchal. You need to buy something for a headache and tell the pharmacist about your allergy.

nodes.npc_text: Boa tarde! Diga, em que posso ajudar?
nodes.npc_translation: Good afternoon! Tell me, how can I help?

options.text: Boa tarde. Dói-me a cabeça.
options.translation: Good afternoon. My head hurts.
options.feedback: You clearly stated your symptom. Good start!

options.text: Tenho uma dor de cabeça.
options.translation: I have a headache.
options.feedback: Correctly described your pain. Well done.

nodes.npc_text: Pois, e tem mais alguma coisa? Febre?
nodes.npc_translation: Right, and do you have anything else? Fever?

options.text: Não, só a cabeça.
options.translation: No, just my head.
options.feedback: Simple and direct. Good!

options.text: Só me dói a cabeça.
options.translation: Only my head hurts.
options.feedback: Clear and concise. Perfect.

nodes.npc_text: OK. Tenho aqui um bom comprimido para a dor de cabeça. Tem alguma alergia?
nodes.npc_translation: Okay. I have a good tablet here for headaches. Do you have any allergies?

options.text: Sim, tenho alergia a aspirina.
options.translation: Yes, I have an allergy to aspirin.
options.feedback: You correctly stated your allergy using the known phrase.

options.text: Sou alérgico a aspirina.
options.translation: I am allergic to aspirin.
options.feedback: Another good way to state your allergy. Excellent.

nodes.npc_text: Ah, OK. Então, este é melhor. É um comprimido. Quer levar?
nodes.npc_translation: Ah, okay. So, this one is better. It's a tablet. Do you want it?

options.text: Sim, por favor. Quantas vezes por dia?
options.translation: Yes, please. How many times a day?
options.feedback: You asked for the dosage, which is important. Good job.

nodes.npc_text: Tome um comprimido, duas vezes por dia, depois das refeições.
nodes.npc_translation: Take one tablet, twice a day, after meals.

options.text: Pois, obrigada!
options.translation: Right, thank you!
options.feedback: You understood the instructions and thanked the pharmacist. Mission accomplished!

options.text: Depois das refeições, certo? Obrigada.
options.translation: After meals, right? Thank you.
options.feedback: You confirmed the instructions, showing good comprehension.

mission.title: Pharmacy Visit: Headache & Allergy
prep: Practice saying 'Dói-me a cabeça' (My head hurts).
Rehearse 'Tenho alergia a aspirina' (I have an allergy to aspirin).
Learn to ask 'Quantas vezes por dia?' (How many times a day?)

situations.title: Stronger Connectors
situations.summary: Add depth to your speech with "portanto", "aliás", and "afinal".

variants.text: A loja estava fechada, portanto fui a outro.
variants.translation: The shop was closed, so I went to another.

variants.text: Não tinham pão, portanto tive de esperar.
variants.translation: They didn't have bread, so I had to wait.

variants.text: Era muito caro, portanto comprei outro.
variants.translation: It was very expensive, so I bought another.

variants.text: Este bolo de mel é ótimo. Aliás, é o melhor da ilha!
variants.translation: This honey cake is great. Actually, it's the best on the island!

variants.text: Não é caro. Aliás, é o mais barato que encontrei.
variants.translation: It's not expensive. In fact, it's the cheapest I found.

variants.text: Não é longe. Aliás, está mesmo aqui ao lado.
variants.translation: It's not far. Actually, it's right next door.

variants.text: Pensei que precisava de ir, mas afinal não era preciso.
variants.translation: I thought I needed to go, but in the end it wasn't necessary.

variants.text: Ele disse que vinha, mas afinal não veio.
variants.translation: He said he was coming, but after all he didn't.

variants.text: Queria ir ao mercado, mas afinal estava fechado.
variants.translation: I wanted to go to the market, but in the end it was closed.

variants.text: Estava a chover, mas mesmo assim gostei do passeio.
variants.translation: It was raining, but even so I enjoyed the walk.

variants.text: Era um bocado caro, mas mesmo assim comprei.
variants.translation: It was a bit expensive, but even so I bought it.

variants.text: Demorou um bocado, mas mesmo assim valeu a pena.
variants.translation: It took a while, but even so it was worth it.

goals: Use sophisticated connectors to link complex thoughts
Express hesitation, contradiction, and emphasis
Sound more like a native speaker with "portanto"

cultural_notes.title: Stronger Connectors
cultural_notes.body: "Portanto" is used constantly in Madeira, often as a filler or thinking pause, similar to "so" in English.

cultural_notes.title: The Versatility of 'Pois'
cultural_notes.body: In Portugal, 'pois' and 'pois é' are extremely common interjections. They can mean 'yes', 'that's right', 'indeed', or simply acknowledge what someone has said, often with a slight pause or a thoughtful tone. It's a very natural way to show you're listening and engaged in a conversation, without necessarily agreeing strongly. It can also be used to prompt someone to continue speaking, much like 'go on...' in English. For example, if someone says 'Fui ao mercado...', you might hear 'Pois...' as an invitation for them to elaborate.

cultural_notes.title: Café Culture in Madeira
cultural_notes.body: Coffee is a fundamental part of daily life in Madeira. When ordering, 'bica' is the common term for a standard espresso, while 'café cheio' is an espresso with more water, making it slightly longer. Knowing these terms and how to use them will make your café experience much smoother and more authentic. Don't be afraid to try different types, like 'garoto' (espresso with a splash of milk) or 'galão' (a larger milk coffee).

dialogues.title: Shopping for bread
dialogues.context: Two friends, Ana and Carlos, are talking about buying bread at the local bakery.

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.text: Fui à padaria buscar pão, mas já não havia.
lines.translation: I went to the bakery to get bread, but there wasn't any left.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.text: Pois é, acontece. Então, o que fizeste?
lines.translation: Yeah, it happens. So, what did you do?

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.text: Portanto fui a outra, mais à frente. Aliás, é o melhor pão de lá!
lines.translation: So I went to another one, further down. Actually, they have the best bread there!

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.text: Ah, bom saber! E afinal, conseguiste pão fresco?
lines.translation: Oh, good to know! And in the end, did you get fresh bread?

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.text: Sim, fresquinho. Mesmo assim gostei da caminhada.
lines.translation: Yes, very fresh. Even so, I enjoyed the walk.

dialogues.title: A trip to the market
dialogues.context: A tourist is asking a local about the market.

lines.speaker: Turista
lines.text: Bom dia! O mercado está aberto hoje?
lines.translation: Good morning! Is the market open today?

lines.speaker: Local
lines.text: Bom dia! Sim, está. Mas afinal, o que procura?
lines.translation: Good morning! Yes, it is. But after all, what are you looking for?

lines.speaker: Turista
lines.text: Queria comprar umas frutas. Pensei em ir ao mercado velho.
lines.translation: I wanted to buy some fruit. I thought about going to the old market.

lines.speaker: Local
lines.text: Ah, o mercado dos Lavradores. Aliás, é o mais bonito!
lines.translation: Ah, the Lavradores market. Actually, it's the most beautiful one!

lines.speaker: Turista
lines.text: Portanto, devo ir lá?
lines.translation: So, I should go there?

lines.speaker: Local
lines.text: Sim, claro. Mesmo assim, prepare-se para as escadas!
lines.translation: Yes, of course. Even so, prepare for the stairs!

roleplay.scenario: You are at a café in Funchal. You wanted a 'bica' (espresso), but they only have 'café cheio' (full espresso). You decide to try it anyway.

nodes.npc_text: Bom dia! O que vai ser?
nodes.npc_translation: Good morning! What will it be?

options.text: Bom dia. Queria uma bica, por favor.
options.translation: Good morning. I'd like an espresso, please.
options.feedback: Good polite start!

nodes.npc_text: Uma bica? Desculpe, só temos café cheio.
nodes.npc_translation: An espresso? Sorry, we only have full espresso.

options.text: Ah, entendi. Portanto, vou querer um café cheio.
options.translation: Ah, I see. So, I'll have a full espresso.
options.feedback: You understood the situation and used 'portanto' to express your decision.

options.text: Afinal, não vou querer nada. Obrigada.
options.translation: In the end, I won't want anything. Thank you.
options.feedback: You changed your mind. 'Afinal' works well here, but maybe you could have tried the 'café cheio'?

nodes.npc_text: Muito bem! Aqui está. Espero que goste.
nodes.npc_translation: Very good! Here it is. I hope you like it.

options.text: Obrigada! É um bocado diferente, mas mesmo assim gostei.
options.translation: Thank you! It's a bit different, but even so I liked it.
options.feedback: Excellent! You used 'mesmo assim' to express your enjoyment despite the difference.

options.text: Obrigada! Aliás, é muito bom!
options.translation: Thank you! Actually, it's very good!
options.feedback: Great! 'Aliás' adds emphasis to your positive surprise. Well done!

prep: Mentally plan your route to the market and then to the pharmacy.
Think about what you'd say if the market was closed or didn't have what you needed.
Practice asking for directions and then changing your plan using 'portanto', 'aliás', or 'afinal'.

cultural_notes.title: Addressing Service Staff
cultural_notes.body: In Portugal, especially in service situations, it's common to use 'o senhor' or 'a senhora' when addressing staff, even if they are younger than you. While 'tu' is widely used among peers, defaulting to the polite form is a safe bet when unsure, or when the interaction is purely transactional. Avoid 'você' unless you're explicitly trying to create distance.

cultural_notes.title: 'Pois' and Agreement
cultural_notes.body: The word 'pois' is very versatile in Portuguese. In Madeira, you'll often hear it, or 'pois é', used to express agreement, affirmation, or simply to acknowledge what someone has said, similar to 'yes, indeed' or 'that's right'. It can also mean 'of course' or 'well'.

dialogues.title: Ordering at a Café
dialogues.context: At a small café in Funchal, ordering coffee and a pastry.

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.text: Bom dia! Queria um café, por favor.
lines.translation: Good morning! I'd like a coffee, please.

lines.speaker: Barista
lines.text: Um café? Mais alguma coisa?
lines.translation: A coffee? Anything else?

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.text: Sim, e um bolo do caco, se faz favor.
lines.translation: Yes, and a bolo do caco, please.

lines.speaker: Barista
lines.text: Pois, tá bem. É para comer aqui ou levar?
lines.translation: Okay, alright. Is it to eat here or take away?

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.text: É para comer aqui, obrigado.
lines.translation: It's to eat here, thank you.

lines.speaker: Barista
lines.text: São três euros e cinquenta.
lines.translation: It's three euros and fifty cents.

dialogues.title: Buying Vegetables at the Market
dialogues.context: At the Mercado dos Lavradores in Funchal, buying vegetables.

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.text: Olá! Tem semilhas hoje?

lines.speaker: Vendor
lines.text: Bom dia! Tenho sim, claro. Quantos quilos quer?

lines.speaker: Vendor

lines.translation: No, thank you. That's all. How much is it?

lines.speaker: Vendor

nodes.npc_text: Um sumo de laranja. Algo mais?

options.text: Não, obrigado. É só isso.

nodes.npc_text: Aqui estão os três. Mais alguma coisa?

options.text: Não, obrigado. É só isso.

options.text: Sim, e um café, por favor.

options.feedback: Adding a coffee, good request!

nodes.npc_text: Pois, tá bem. Bom dia!

nodes.npc_text: Fica a quatro euros e setenta. Obrigado!

nodes.npc_text: São três euros e sessenta. Obrigado!

nodes.npc_text: Um café. Algo mais?
nodes.npc_translation: A coffee. Anything else?

options.text: Não, obrigado. É só isso.
options.translation: No, thank you. That's all.

nodes.npc_text: Fica a quatro euros e oitenta. Obrigado!

situations.title: The Imperfect Tense
situations.summary: Describe ongoing past states, habitual past actions, and how things used to be.

variants.translation: I was hungry when I arrived.

variants.text: Estava muito feliz quando te vi.
variants.translation: I was very happy when I saw you.

variants.text: Estava com sono quando o filme acabou.
variants.translation: I was sleepy when the movie ended.

variants.text: Antes ia à praia todos os dias.
variants.translation: Before, I used to go to the beach every day.
Before, I used to study a lot.

phrase_patterns.base: Estava a ler quando o telefone tocou.

variants.translation: I was cooking when you arrived.

variants.text: Estava a ver televisão quando começou a chover.
variants.translation: I was watching TV when it started to rain.

variants.text: Estava a dormir quando ele ligou.
variants.translation: I was sleeping when he called.

goals: Master the imperfect tense
Contrast preterite and imperfect
Use havia correctly

cultural_notes.title: The Imperfect Tense
cultural_notes.body: The imperfect describes ongoing past states or habits. Contrast it with the preterite (specific events).

cultural_notes.title: Using 'Pois' in Conversation
cultural_notes.body: 'Pois' and 'Pois é' are common interjections in Portuguese, especially in Madeira. They can mean 'indeed', 'that's right', 'I see', or simply acknowledge what the other person said, showing you're listening. It's a very natural way to participate in a conversation without necessarily adding new information.

cultural_notes.title: Describing the Weather in the Past
cultural_notes.body: When talking about past weather, the imperfect tense is almost always used. For example, 'Estava sol' (It was sunny), 'Chovia muito' (It was raining a lot), 'Fazia frio' (It was cold). This describes the general conditions rather than a specific, completed event.

dialogues.title: Old Habits
dialogues.context: Two friends, Ana and Carlos, are talking about their past routines in a café.

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.text: Então, Carlos, como era a tua vida antes de vires p'ra Madeira?
lines.translation: So, Carlos, what was your life like before you came to Madeira?

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.text: Ah, era diferente. Antes, trabalhava num escritório e ia de autocarro todos os dias.
lines.translation: Ah, it was different. Before, I worked in an office and went by bus every day.

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.text: Pois, e o que fazias nos fins de semana?
lines.translation: Right, and what did you do on the weekends?

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.text: Geralmente, ficava em casa a ler ou visitava os meus pais. Não havia tanto sol como aqui!
lines.translation: Generally, I stayed at home reading or visited my parents. There wasn't as much sun as here!

dialogues.title: A Rainy Day Memory
dialogues.context: Maria and João are remembering a past event.

lines.speaker: Maria
lines.text: Lembram-te daquela vez que estávamos na levada e começou a chover?
lines.translation: Do you remember that time we were on the levada and it started to rain?

lines.speaker: João
lines.text: Claro! Eu estava a tirar fotografias quando a chuva caiu forte. Foi uma aventura!
lines.translation: Of course! I was taking photos when the rain fell heavily. It was an adventure!

lines.speaker: Maria
lines.text: Sim, e tu estavas com um chapéu-de-chuva, mas não serviu de nada!
lines.translation: Yes, and you had an umbrella, but it was no use!

lines.speaker: João
lines.text: Pois é! Ficámos os dois molhados. Mas era divertido, não era?
lines.translation: That's right! We both got soaked. But it was fun, wasn't it?

roleplay.scenario: You are talking to a new neighbor in Madeira, who is asking about your life before moving here.

nodes.npc_text: Olá! Então, antes de vires p'ra Madeira, onde moravas?
nodes.npc_translation: Hello! So, before you came to Madeira, where did you live?

options.text: Morava no Porto.
options.translation: I lived in Porto.
options.feedback: Good use of 'morava' for a past habitual state.

options.text: Estava no Porto.
options.translation: I was in Porto.
options.feedback: Correct, but 'morava' is more specific for 'lived'.

nodes.npc_text: Ah, Porto! E o que fazias lá? Trabalhavas ou estudavas?
nodes.npc_translation: Ah, Porto! And what did you do there? Did you work or study?

options.text: Trabalhava num banco.
options.translation: I worked in a bank.
options.feedback: Excellent! 'Trabalhava' for habitual past action.

options.text: Estudava na universidade.
options.translation: I studied at the university.
options.feedback: Perfect! 'Estudava' for a past ongoing activity.

nodes.npc_text: Pois. E o que fazias no Porto? Qual era a tua rotina?
nodes.npc_translation: Right. And what did you do in Porto? What was your routine?

options.text: Trabalhava num banco e ia ao ginásio.
options.translation: I worked in a bank and went to the gym.
options.feedback: Very good! You used two imperfect verbs for past habits.

options.text: Estudava e saía com amigos.
options.translation: I studied and went out with friends.
options.feedback: Well done! 'Estudava' and 'saía' describe past routines.

nodes.npc_text: Interessante! E havia muitos turistas no Porto, não havia?
nodes.npc_translation: Interesting! And there were a lot of tourists in Porto, weren't there?

options.text: Sim, havia sempre muitos.
options.translation: Yes, there were always many.
options.feedback: Correct use of 'havia' for 'there was/were' in the past.

options.text: Não, não havia tantos como agora.
options.translation: No, there weren't as many as now.
options.feedback: Good job with the negative form of 'havia'.

mission.title: Share a Past Memory
prep: Think of a simple past memory from your childhood or a previous place you lived.
Practice describing a habitual action or state using the imperfect tense (e.g., 'Eu brincava...', 'Havia...', 'Era...').
Practice describing an ongoing action interrupted by another event (e.g., 'Eu estava a fazer... quando...').
likely_responses: Pois é, a vida era diferente.

situations.title: The Future: Going To & Will
situations.summary: Speak about the future confidently using ir + infinitive and simple future forms.

variants.translation: Tomorrow we're going to the market.
Later I'm going to the café.
I will do the shopping later.
We intend to learn Portuguese this year.
Do you intend to swim later?
I intend to do sports soon.

dialogues.title: Market Plans
dialogues.context: Two neighbours, Maria and Ana, meet in the street and discuss plans for the market.

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.text: Olá, Maria! Vais ao mercado amanhã?
lines.translation: Hi, Maria! Are you going to the market tomorrow?

lines.speaker: Maria
lines.text: Pois é, vou sim. Preciso de fruta e semilhas.
lines.translation: Yes, I am. I need fruit and potatoes.

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.text: Eu também irei. Encontramo-nos lá?
lines.translation: I will go too. Shall we meet there?

lines.speaker: Maria
lines.text: Combinado! Até amanhã!
lines.translation: Agreed! See you tomorrow!

dialogues.title: Evening Plans
dialogues.context: Two friends, Pedro and João, chat about their evening plans at a café.

lines.speaker: Pedro
lines.text: Então, o que vais fazer logo?
lines.translation: So, what are you going to do later?

lines.speaker: João
lines.text: Logo, irei estudar um bocado. E tu?
lines.translation: Later, I'll study a bit. And you?

lines.speaker: Pedro
lines.text: Eu vou ver a bola com os meus amigos. Depois, talvez uma poncha.
lines.translation: I'm going to watch the football with my friends. Afterwards, maybe a poncha.

lines.speaker: João
lines.text: Ah, boa! Diverte-te!
lines.translation: Ah, good! Have fun!

roleplay.scenario: You're having a bica (coffee) at a café in Funchal, and a friend asks about your plans for tomorrow.

nodes.npc_text: Olá! Então, o que vais fazer amanhã?
nodes.npc_translation: Hi! So, what are you going to do tomorrow?

options.text: Amanhã vou ao mercado.
options.translation: Tomorrow I'm going to the market.
options.feedback: Great use of 'ir + infinitive' to state a plan.

options.text: Não sei bem, vou descansar um bocado.
options.translation: I don't really know, I'm going to rest a bit.
options.feedback: A natural way to express uncertainty and a simple future action.

options.text: Tenciono aprender português.
options.translation: I intend to learn Portuguese.
options.feedback: Good use of 'tencionar' for an intention.

nodes.npc_text: Ah, que bom! Eu também preciso de ir. Encontramo-nos lá, talvez para uma bica?
nodes.npc_translation: Oh, how good! I also need to go. Shall we meet there, maybe for a coffee?

options.text: Sim, claro! Amanhã de manhã.
options.translation: Yes, of course! Tomorrow morning.
options.feedback: Perfect! You've agreed to meet.

options.text: Não, obrigado. Prefiro ir sozinho.
options.translation: No, thank you. I prefer to go alone.
options.feedback: Polite refusal, well done.

nodes.npc_text: Pois é, às vezes é preciso. Eu vou trabalhar, infelizmente.
nodes.npc_translation: Indeed, sometimes it's necessary. I'm going to work, unfortunately.

options.text: Que pena! Bom trabalho!
options.translation: What a pity! Good work!
options.feedback: A sympathetic and common phrase.

options.text: Eu farei isso logo, depois de arrumar a casa.
options.translation: I will do that later, after tidying the house.
options.feedback: Good use of the simple future and 'logo'.

nodes.npc_text: Excelente! É uma língua muito bonita. Vais gostar, pois é um desafio!
nodes.npc_translation: Excellent! It's a very beautiful language. You will like it, as it's a challenge!

options.text: Pois é. Estou entusiasmado.
options.translation: Indeed. I'm excited.
options.feedback: A natural and positive response.

options.text: Sim, e tenciono praticar imenso.
options.translation: Yes, and I intend to practice a lot.
options.feedback: Good use of 'tencionar' and 'imenso' (a lot).

mission.title: Share Your Plans
prep: Think of two things you will do this week.
Practice saying 'Vou...' and 'Farei...' for these plans.
Imagine someone asking you 'O que vais fazer...?'

cultural_notes.title: Future Tense Usage in Madeira
cultural_notes.body: In daily conversation in Madeira, and Portugal in general, the construction 'ir + infinitive' (e.g., 'vou fazer', 'I'm going to do') is very common for expressing future plans or intentions, especially for the near future. While the simple future tense (e.g., 'farei', 'I will do') is grammatically correct and used, it can sometimes sound a bit more formal or literary in casual speech. Both are understood and correct, but 'ir + infinitive' often feels more natural in informal contexts.

cultural_notes.title: The Art of Planning in Madeira
cultural_notes.body: Discussions about future plans are a common part of social interactions in Madeira, often happening over a 'bica' (espresso) or a meal. Plans can be made quite casually, and it's common to agree to meet 'depois' (later) or 'amanhã' (tomorrow) without always setting a very rigid time. Flexibility is often appreciated.

situations.title: Wishes, Conditionals & Polite Requests
situations.summary: Express wishes, hypotheticals, and polished polite requests.

variants.text: Gostava de comer um bolo do caco.

I would like to drink a poncha.

lines.translation: Yes, it's for here. And I'd like to have more time to stay in Madeira!

options.text: Gostava de ficar mais tempo aqui no mercado.

options.text: Se tivesse mais dinheiro, comprava tudo!

mission.title: Expressing Wishes and Polite Requests
prep: Practice using 'Gostava de...' for polite requests.

cultural_notes.title: Politeness with 'Gostava de...'
cultural_notes.body: In Portugal, 'Gostava de...' (literally 'I would like to...') is a very common and polite way to express a wish or make a request, much like 'I would like' or 'I'd like' in English. It is generally preferred over 'Queria...' ('I wanted...') which can sound a bit more direct or demanding in some contexts, especially when ordering.

cultural_notes.title: The Versatile 'Pois'
cultural_notes.body: 'Pois' is a very common and versatile word in Portuguese, especially in Portugal. It can mean 'well', 'so', 'indeed', or 'that's right'. Often, 'Pois é' is used to agree with someone or acknowledge what they've said, similar to 'That's true' or 'Yeah' in English. It adds a natural, conversational flow to dialogue.

cultural_notes.title: Directness in Ordering
cultural_notes.body: In Portuguese restaurants, it's common and polite to be quite direct when ordering. Phrases like 'Queria...' (I would like...) or 'Para mim...' (For me...) followed by the dish are perfectly acceptable and do not sound rude. There's no need for overly elaborate requests.

cultural_notes.title: The Versatile 'Pois é'
cultural_notes.body: 'Pois é' is a very common expression in Portugal, especially in Madeira, used to express agreement, acknowledgement, or to confirm something someone just said. It's similar to 'Indeed,' 'That's right,' or 'You know it.' Using it naturally can make your Portuguese sound more authentic.

mission.title: Describe Your Home to a New Acquaintance
prep: Think about your home: Is it an apartment or a house? Is it big or small?
Decide which floor it's on (e.g., first, second, ground floor).
Consider your neighbourhood: Is it quiet, central, or does it have a view?
Practice saying 'Vivo num apartamento pequeno', 'Fica no terceiro andar', and 'É uma zona sossegada'.

cultural_notes.title: Floor Numbers in Portugal
cultural_notes.body: In Portugal, the ground floor is called 'rés-do-chão' (literally 'ground-floor'). The floor above it is the 'primeiro andar' (first floor), then 'segundo andar' (second floor), and so on. This is different from systems where the ground floor is considered the 'first floor'.

cultural_notes.title: Using 'Vivo' vs 'Moro'
cultural_notes.body: Both 'Vivo' (from 'viver') and 'Moro' (from 'morar') mean 'I live'. They are often interchangeable in daily conversation in Portugal. 'Viver' can also mean 'to be alive', while 'morar' is exclusively about residence. Feel free to use either at this level.

lines.speaker: Turista
lines.text: Olá! Queria saber, quantas pessoas vivem na Madeira?

lines.speaker: Turista

dialogues.title: Buying Bolo do Caco
dialogues.context: At a market stall selling traditional Madeiran food.

lines.speaker: Cliente
lines.text: Bom dia, minha senhora. Quanto é que custa o bolo do caco?

lines.speaker: Vendedora
lines.text: Bom dia! Cada um é um euro e cinquenta. Mas se levar cem, faço-lhe um preço melhor.
lines.translation: Good morning! Each one is one euro fifty. But if you take a hundred, I'll give you a better price.

lines.translation: A hundred? No, I don't need that many! I just wanted two or three. Thank you.

prep: Practice saying numbers like 'cem', 'duzentos', 'mil'.
Practice asking 'Quanto custa...?' (How much does... cost?)

likely_responses: Se levar Z, faço-lhe um preço melhor.

cultural_notes.title: Saying Years in Portuguese
cultural_notes.body: When saying years in Portuguese, especially those before 2000, each part of the number is often pronounced. For example, 1985 is 'mil novecentos e oitenta e cinco' (one thousand nine hundred and eighty-five), not 'nineteen eighty-five' as in English. For years after 2000, like 2023, it's 'dois mil e vinte e três'.

cultural_notes.title: Using 'de' with 'milhão'
cultural_notes.body: When using 'milhão' (million), it is almost always followed by 'de' before the noun, even if the noun is not explicitly stated but implied. For example, 'um milhão de pessoas' (a million people), or 'um milhão e duzentos mil' (a million two hundred thousand - 'de' is implied before the 'mil'). For numbers like 'cem mil' (one hundred thousand) or 'duzentas mil' (two hundred thousand), 'de' is not used before the noun unless the noun is plural and indefinite (e.g., 'cem mil de bananas' - which is less common than 'cem mil bananas').

cultural_notes.title: Bica vs. Café
cultural_notes.body: In Portugal, a 'bica' is a small, strong espresso, often served in a small cup. While 'café' is also understood, 'bica' is very common, especially in Lisbon and Madeira, and using it sounds more natural and local. It's a key part of daily life.

cultural_notes.title: Addressing Shopkeepers
cultural_notes.body: When entering a small shop or café, it's polite to greet the shopkeeper with 'Bom dia' (Good morning), 'Boa tarde' (Good afternoon), or 'Boa noite' (Good evening). Using 'se faz favor' or 'por favor' when asking for items is essential. You'll often hear 'Até já' (See you soon) when leaving, even if you don't expect to return immediately, as it's a common friendly farewell.

cultural_notes.title: Navigating 'Tu' vs. 'O Senhor/A Senhora'
cultural_notes.body: In Portugal, 'tu' (informal 'you') is used with family, friends, children, and often with people of similar age in casual settings like cafés or shops. 'O senhor' (the gentleman) / 'A senhora' (the lady) are used as formal ways to say 'you' when addressing older people, professionals, or anyone you want to show respect to, especially in a first interaction. When in doubt, start with 'o senhor' / 'a senhora' to be safe. People might invite you to use 'tu' if they feel comfortable.

cultural_notes.title: The Importance of Context and Age
cultural_notes.body: The choice between 'tu' and 'o senhor/a senhora' is highly dependent on context and perceived age. If you're addressing someone significantly older than you, or in a professional setting (bank, public office), 'o senhor/a senhora' is almost always appropriate. With someone your age or younger, especially in a relaxed environment, 'tu' is generally fine. Pay attention to how the other person addresses you; often, they will set the tone.

cultural_notes.title: 'A gente' vs 'Nós'
cultural_notes.body: In spoken Portuguese, especially in informal contexts like Madeira, 'a gente' (literally 'the people' but meaning 'we') is very commonly used instead of 'nós'. It's often preferred because it's slightly more informal and conversational. The verb that follows 'a gente' is always conjugated in the third person singular, like 'ele/ela' (e.g., 'a gente gosta', 'a gente vai').

cultural_notes.title: Café Culture in Madeira
cultural_notes.body: Going to a café is a daily ritual for many Madeirans. It's not just about drinking coffee; it's a social hub where people meet friends, read the newspaper, or simply watch the world go by. It's common to order a 'bica' (espresso) and perhaps a 'bolo do caco' (a traditional Madeiran bread).

cultural_notes.title: Using 'Pois' for agreement
cultural_notes.body: In Portugal, 'Pois' or 'Pois é' is commonly used as a conversational filler to express agreement, understanding, or to acknowledge what someone has said. It's similar to 'Indeed' or 'That's right' in English and is very common in everyday speech, especially in Madeira.

cultural_notes.title: 'Um bocado' vs. 'Um pouco'
cultural_notes.body: While 'um pouco' is grammatically correct and understood, in Madeira and much of Portugal, 'um bocado' is very frequently used to mean 'a little bit' or 'a bit'. For instance, 'Estávamos um bocado cansados' (We were a bit tired) sounds very natural and local.

prep: Write down your name and a fake health number (número de utente).
Practice saying 'Queria marcar uma consulta.'
Practice saying 'O meu número de utente é...' followed by a number.

cultural_notes.title: Phone Etiquette in Portugal
cultural_notes.body: When making a formal call in Portugal, especially to a clinic or service, it's customary to start with a greeting like 'Bom dia' (Good morning) or 'Boa tarde' (Good afternoon) even if the other person answers first. Always use polite forms like 'por favor' (please) and 'obrigado/a' (thank you). The polite 'o senhor/a senhora' form is implied or used, even if not explicitly stated as a pronoun.

cultural_notes.title: The 'Número de Utente' (Health Number)
cultural_notes.body: In Portugal, the 'número de utente' is your unique health service identifier. It's essential for accessing public healthcare, booking appointments, and picking up prescriptions. You will almost always be asked for it when interacting with medical services.

Say this phrase from memory: "Queria marcar uma consulta."
Say this phrase from memory: "O meu número de utente é..."
review_items.answer: O meu número de utente é...

situations.title: Opinions: Agreeing & Disagreeing
situations.summary: Agree enthusiastically, disagree politely, and express nuanced views.

phrase_patterns.base: Acho que não é bem assim.

variants.translation: It doesn't seem quite right to me.

dialogues.title: Planning a trip
dialogues.context: Two friends, Ana and Rui, are discussing plans for a weekend trip to the north of the island.

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.text: Acho que ir p'ra Santana no sábado é a melhor opção. O tempo lá é sempre bom.
lines.translation: I think going to Santana on Saturday is the best option. The weather there is always good.

lines.speaker: Rui
lines.text: Hum... Acho que não é bem assim. No sábado, pode estar nublado lá. Tens razão sobre a beleza, mas o tempo...
lines.translation: Hmm... I don't think that's quite right. On Saturday, it might be cloudy there. You're right about the beauty, but the weather...

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.text: Pois, depende. Mas no domingo não é melhor. Concordo totalmente que o tempo é importante, mas o sábado é mais livre.
lines.translation: Well, it depends. But Sunday isn't better. I totally agree that the weather is important, but Saturday is freer.

lines.speaker: Rui
lines.text: Exatamente! O tempo lá em Santana é imprevisível. Que tal Porto Moniz?
lines.translation: Exactly! The weather there in Santana is unpredictable. How about Porto Moniz?

dialogues.title: Coffee and opinions
dialogues.context: Two neighbours, Dona Rosa and Senhor Manuel, are having a coffee at a local café.

lines.speaker: Dona Rosa
lines.text: Este café está mesmo bom hoje. Acho que é o melhor da zona.
lines.translation: This coffee is really good today. I think it's the best in the area.

lines.speaker: Senhor Manuel
lines.text: Tens razão, Dona Rosa. Concordo totalmente. O rapaz novo faz o café muito bem.
lines.translation: You're right, Dona Rosa. I totally agree. The new boy makes the coffee very well.

lines.speaker: Dona Rosa
lines.text: Sim, mas os preços estão a subir, não estão? Acho que não é bem assim, subir tanto.
lines.translation: Yes, but the prices are going up, aren't they? I don't think it's quite right, to go up so much.

lines.speaker: Senhor Manuel
lines.text: Ah, pois, depende. A vida está mais cara para todos. Mas o café é bom, isso é o que importa.
lines.translation: Ah, well, it depends. Life is more expensive for everyone. But the coffee is good, that's what matters.

roleplay.scenario: You are at a local market in Funchal, talking to a seller about the quality of some fruit.

nodes.npc_text: Bom dia! Estas bananas são as melhores da ilha, garanto-lhe. Fresquinhas, acabadas de colher!
nodes.npc_translation: Good morning! These bananas are the best on the island, I guarantee you. Fresh, just picked!

options.text: Concordo totalmente, parecem ótimas!
options.translation: I totally agree, they look great!
options.feedback: Great! You agreed enthusiastically and politely.

options.text: Acho que não é bem assim, as outras também são boas.
options.translation: I don't think that's quite right, the others are good too.
options.feedback: You've politely disagreed. See what the seller says next.

options.text: Depende, não é?
options.translation: It depends, doesn't it?
options.feedback: A good way to express a nuanced view. Let's hear the seller's response.

nodes.npc_text: Pois é! Leve um cacho, não se vai arrepender! Quer mais alguma coisa?
nodes.npc_translation: That's right! Take a bunch, you won't regret it! Do you want anything else?

options.text: Não, obrigado. Só as bananas, por favor.
options.translation: No, thank you. Just the bananas, please.
options.feedback: You've completed the interaction, buying the bananas.

nodes.npc_text: Ah, pois, mas estas são biológicas e mais doces! Não há comparação, digo eu.
nodes.npc_translation: Ah, well, but these are organic and sweeter! There's no comparison, I say.

options.text: Tens razão, talvez. Vou levar umas.
options.translation: You're right, maybe. I'll take some.
options.feedback: You've acknowledged their point and decided to buy.

options.text: Acho que não é bem assim. Vou ver as outras bancas.
options.translation: I don't think that's quite right. I'll check the other stalls.
options.feedback: You've decided to look elsewhere. A polite way to end the conversation.

nodes.npc_text: Depende do quê? Da estação? Do sol? Estas são sempre boas!
nodes.npc_translation: Depends on what? The season? The sun? These are always good!

options.text: Depende do gosto de cada um, não é? Mas parecem boas.
options.translation: It depends on everyone's taste, doesn't it? But they look good.
options.feedback: You explained your nuanced view and still showed interest.

options.text: Tens razão, talvez. Vou levar umas para experimentar.
options.translation: You're right, maybe. I'll take some to try.
options.feedback: You've decided to give them a try.

nodes.npc_text: Muito bem! Não se vai arrepender! Quer mais alguma coisa?
nodes.npc_translation: Very good! You won't regret it! Do you want anything else?

options.text: Não, obrigado. Só as bananas.
options.translation: No, thank you. Just the bananas.
options.feedback: You've successfully completed the purchase.

mission.title: Discuss a local event
prep: Think of a recent local event or news item in Madeira (e.g., a festival, a new shop, weather).
Practice saying 'Concordo totalmente' and 'Acho que não é bem assim' with different tones.
Prepare to ask a simple opinion question, like 'O que acha disto?' (What do you think of this?)

cultural_notes.title: Politeness in Disagreement
cultural_notes.body: In Portugal, direct disagreement can sometimes be seen as impolite, especially with strangers or in formal settings. Phrases like 'Acho que não é bem assim' (I don't think it's quite like that) or 'Não me parece' (It doesn't seem so to me) are softer ways to express a different opinion without being confrontational. 'Depende' (It depends) is also a very common and polite way to avoid taking a strong stance.

cultural_notes.title: Using 'Pois' for Agreement and Nuance
cultural_notes.body: The word 'Pois' is extremely common in Portuguese and can mean 'indeed', 'well', or 'that's right'. It's often used to acknowledge what someone has said, often indicating agreement ('Pois é!') or to introduce a nuanced point after someone has spoken. It's a versatile interjection that adds a natural flow to conversations, especially in Madeira.

Say this phrase from memory: "Acho que não é bem assim."
review_items.answer: Acho que não é bem assim.

variants.note: Adds a touch of hesitancy or suggestion.
